title: Cat Straining to Urinate: Emergency Triage Guide for Pet Owners
meta_desc: Is your cat straining in the litter box? Learn why this is a high-priority emergency and how to identify life-threatening urinary blockages.
content_html:
TL;DR: Urinary straining in cats is a life-threatening medical emergency that requires immediate veterinary evaluation to prevent complications like kidney failure or a ruptured bladder. If your cat is straining or unable to urinate, contact an emergency veterinarian immediately.

What is urinary straining in cats and what are the symptoms?

Urinary straining, medically known as dysuria, occurs when a cat makes frequent, painful attempts to urinate. While this behavior is often confused with constipation, it is frequently a sign of a serious urinary tract issue. Common signs include:

  • Squatting for long periods in the litter box
  • Crying out while attempting to urinate
  • Producing only tiny droplets of urine
  • Urine that is tinged with blood

Is urinary straining in cats considered a medical emergency?

Yes. This is considered a High Urgency situation because a cat with a urinary blockage is unable to eliminate toxins from their body. If left untreated, this can lead to the following within 24 to 48 hours:

  • Kidney failure
  • A ruptured bladder
  • Life-threatening heart arrhythmias

While it is most common in male cats due to their narrow anatomy, any cat showing these signs should be evaluated by a veterinarian immediately.

How can providing a photo or video help the veterinary triage process?

When you contact an emergency vet, providing visual evidence can be vital in helping the triage team quickly differentiate between a partial and total obstruction. You should provide:

  • A photo of the litter box showing the size and color of any urine clumps.
  • A short video of your cat's straining behavior.
  • Any other visuals that allow the team to prioritize your cat's care the moment you arrive at the clinic.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

In cats, straining to urinate, or dysuria, may manifest as frequent attempts to urinate, producing only a fine stream, a few drops, or nothing, and may be accompanied by crying out in pain. Complete urethral obstruction is an emergency, leading to uremia within 36-48 hours, with subsequent depression, anorexia, vomiting, diarrhea, dehydration, coma, and potential death within 48-72 hours if untreated. Urolithiasis, urinary tract infection, neoplasia, trauma, urethral plugs, and sterile cystitis (feline interstitial cystitis) are associated conditions. Matrix-crystalline plugs are commonly found in the urethra and are a primary cause of urethral obstruction; calcium oxalate uroliths are also increasingly prevalent. Urinalysis, urine culture, radiography, and ultrasonography are important diagnostics.

Chapter: Urology, Emergency

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 1527)

faqs.question: Can I wait until tomorrow to see my regular vet?
faqs.answer: If your cat is unable to pass urine, you cannot wait. An obstruction is a painful, time-sensitive emergency that requires immediate intervention.

faqs.question: Is straining always a sign of a blockage?
faqs.answer: Not always; it could be cystitis or a urinary tract infection. However, without a physical exam, it is impossible to tell the difference between an infection and a fatal blockage.

faqs.question: What are other red-flag symptoms to look for?
faqs.answer: Watch for vomiting, extreme lethargy, hiding, or a hard, painful belly. These are signs that the condition has progressed to a critical stage.
